_meta.description: Sample data for Graft — 'Git for agent conversations'
_meta.scenario: A developer named Alex builds a recipe-sharing web app called 'RecipeBox' across 6 coding sessions, one per feature. The data showcases branching, pinning, importing, summarization, divergence, and full-text search.
_meta.note_on_ancestry: The node_ancestry table below shows a representative subset. In production, the closure-table trigger auto-populates all (ancestor, descendant, depth) pairs on every INSERT into nodes.

nodes.content: You are an expert full-stack developer. Help the user build production-quality web applications using FastAPI, PostgreSQL, and React. Write clean, well-structured code with proper error handling.

I'm starting a new project called RecipeBox — a recipe sharing web app where users can create, share, and discover recipes. Let's set it up with FastAPI and PostgreSQL. Give me the project scaffold with proper directory structure.

Here's the RecipeBox project scaffold:

```
recipebox/
├── app/
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── main.py            # FastAPI entry point
│   ├── config.py          # Settings via pydantic-settings
│   ├── database.py        # SQLAlchemy engine + session
│   ├── models/            # ORM models
│   ├── schemas/           # Pydantic request/response
│   ├── routers/           # API route modules
│   └── services/          # Business logic
├── alembic/               # DB migrations
├── tests/
├── requirements.txt
└── docker-compose.yml     # Postgres + app
```

I've set up SQLAlchemy 2.0 with async sessions, Alembic for migrations, and a docker-compose with PostgreSQL 16. The config reads DATABASE_URL from environment.
Now let's define the database schema. I need tables for users, recipes, ingredients, and categories. Recipes belong to users and categories.

Here's the core schema:

```sql
CREATE TABLE users (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    username VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    password_hash VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    bio TEXT,
    avatar_url TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE categories (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    slug VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL
);

CREATE TABLE recipes (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    author_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    category_id UUID REFERENCES categories(id),
    title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    description TEXT,
    instructions TEXT NOT NULL,
    prep_time_min INT,
    cook_time_min INT,
    servings INT,
    difficulty VARCHAR(20) CHECK (difficulty IN ('easy','medium','hard')),
    is_published BOOLEAN DEFAULT false,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE ingredients (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    name VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL
);
```

Indexes on `recipes.author_id`, `recipes.category_id`, and `recipes.created_at` for listing queries. The `users` table stores password hashes — we'll use bcrypt when we build auth.
Good, but we also need a recipe_ingredients junction table with quantity and unit, and a reviews table for user ratings.

Added the junction and reviews tables:

```sql
CREATE TABLE recipe_ingredients (
    recipe_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES recipes(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    ingredient_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES ingredients(id),
    quantity DECIMAL(10,2),
    unit VARCHAR(30),
    notes VARCHAR(200),
    display_order INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    PRIMARY KEY (recipe_id, ingredient_id)
);

CREATE TABLE reviews (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    recipe_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES recipes(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    reviewer_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    rating SMALLINT NOT NULL CHECK (rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5),
    comment TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
    UNIQUE (recipe_id, reviewer_id)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_reviews_recipe ON reviews(recipe_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_reviews_reviewer ON reviews(reviewer_id);
```

`recipe_ingredients` uses a composite PK and includes `display_order` so ingredient lists render in the right order. `reviews` enforces one review per user per recipe with a UNIQUE constraint. The schema now has 6 tables: users, categories, recipes, ingredients, recipe_ingredients, reviews.

nodes._session: Session 2 — Authentication (May 2, feat/auth branch, forked from n-07)
nodes.content: Let's build user authentication. I want JWT access tokens with bcrypt password hashing. Include signup, login, and a /me endpoint.
Add email verification. After signup, users get a verification email with a token link. Unverified users can't create recipes.

Email verification implementation:

```python
# Added to users table
# is_verified BOOLEAN DEFAULT false
# verification_token UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
# token_expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ

# app/services/email.py
async def send_verification_email(user: User):
    token = user.verification_token
    link = f'{settings.frontend_url}/verify?token={token}'
    await send_email(
        to=user.email,
        subject='Verify your RecipeBox account',
        body=f'Click to verify: {link}'
    )

# app/routers/auth.py
@router.get('/verify')
async def verify_email(token: UUID, db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)):
    user = await db.execute(
        select(User).where(
            User.verification_token == token,
            User.token_expires_at > datetime.utcnow()
        )
    )
    user = user.scalar_one_or_none()
    if not user:
        raise HTTPException(400, 'Invalid or expired token')
    user.is_verified = True
    user.verification_token = None
    await db.commit()
    return {'message': 'Email verified'}
```

I also added a `require_verified` dependency that checks `current_user.is_verified` and returns 403 if not. Use it on recipe creation endpoints.
Now add Google OAuth so users can sign up / log in with their Google account as an alternative to email+password.

Google OAuth integration using authlib:

```python
# app/services/oauth.py
from authlib.integrations.starlette_client import OAuth

oauth = OAuth()
oauth.register(
    name='google',
    client_id=settings.google_client_id,
    client_secret=settings.google_client_secret,
    server_metadata_url='https://accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration',
    client_kwargs={'scope': 'openid email profile'}
)

# app/routers/auth.py
@router.get('/login/google')
async def google_login(request: Request):
    redirect_uri = request.url_for('google_callback')
    return await oauth.google.authorize_redirect(request, redirect_uri)

@router.get('/callback/google')
async def google_callback(request: Request, db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)):
    token = await oauth.google.authorize_access_token(request)
    user_info = token.get('userinfo')
    
    user = await get_or_create_oauth_user(
        db, email=user_info['email'],
        name=user_info['name'],
        provider='google',
        provider_id=user_info['sub']
    )
    user.is_verified = True  # Google already verified the email
    await db.commit()
    return {'access_token': create_access_token(str(user.id))}
```

I added an `oauth_provider` and `oauth_provider_id` column to the users table. If a user with the same email already exists (signed up via password), we link the accounts. Google-authenticated users are automatically marked as verified.

nodes._session: Session 3 — Recipe CRUD (May 3, feat/recipe-crud branch, forked from n-07)
nodes.content: Let's build the recipe CRUD API. I need endpoints to create, read, update, and delete recipes with proper input validation.

Here are the Recipe Pydantic schemas and CRUD endpoints:

```python
# app/schemas/recipe.py
class RecipeCreate(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(max_length=200)
    description: str | None = None
    instructions: str
    category_id: UUID | None = None
    prep_time_min: int = Field(ge=0, le=1440)
    cook_time_min: int = Field(ge=0, le=1440)
    servings: int = Field(ge=1, le=100)
    difficulty: Literal['easy', 'medium', 'hard']
    ingredients: list[IngredientInput]

class RecipeResponse(BaseModel):
    id: UUID
    title: str
    description: str | None
    author: UserSummary
    category: CategoryResponse | None
    ingredients: list[IngredientResponse]
    prep_time_min: int
    cook_time_min: int
    servings: int
    difficulty: str
    avg_rating: float | None
    created_at: datetime

# app/routers/recipes.py
@router.post('/', status_code=201)
async def create_recipe(
    body: RecipeCreate,
    user: User = Depends(get_current_user),
    db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)
):
    recipe = Recipe(**body.model_dump(exclude={'ingredients'}), author_id=user.id)
    db.add(recipe)
    for ing in body.ingredients:
        ingredient = await get_or_create_ingredient(db, ing.name)
        recipe_ing = RecipeIngredient(
            recipe_id=recipe.id, ingredient_id=ingredient.id,
            quantity=ing.quantity, unit=ing.unit, display_order=ing.order
        )
        db.add(recipe_ing)
    await db.commit()
    return await get_recipe_with_relations(db, recipe.id)

@router.get('/{recipe_id}')
async def get_recipe(recipe_id: UUID, db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)):
    recipe = await get_recipe_with_relations(db, recipe_id)
    if not recipe:
        raise HTTPException(404)
    return recipe

@router.put('/{recipe_id}')
async def update_recipe(
    recipe_id: UUID, body: RecipeUpdate,
    user: User = Depends(get_current_user),
    db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)
):
    recipe = await get_recipe_or_404(db, recipe_id)
    if recipe.author_id != user.id:
        raise HTTPException(403)
    for field, value in body.model_dump(exclude_unset=True).items():
        setattr(recipe, field, value)
    await db.commit()
    return await get_recipe_with_relations(db, recipe.id)

@router.delete('/{recipe_id}', status_code=204)
async def delete_recipe(
    recipe_id: UUID,
    user: User = Depends(get_current_user),
    db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)
):
    recipe = await get_recipe_or_404(db, recipe_id)
    if recipe.author_id != user.id:
        raise HTTPException(403)
    await db.delete(recipe)
    await db.commit()
```

The `get_recipe_with_relations` helper uses `selectinload` for eager-loading ingredients, category, and author in a single query. `RecipeUpdate` is a partial model (all fields optional) so you can PATCH individual fields.
Add cursor-based pagination to the recipe listing, and filtering by category, maximum cook time, and difficulty level.

Cursor-based pagination with multi-field filtering:

```python
# app/schemas/recipe.py
class RecipeListParams(BaseModel):
    cursor: UUID | None = None
    limit: int = Field(default=20, ge=1, le=100)
    category_slug: str | None = None
    max_cook_time: int | None = Field(default=None, ge=0)
    difficulty: Literal['easy', 'medium', 'hard'] | None = None
    sort_by: Literal['created_at', 'avg_rating', 'cook_time'] = 'created_at'

# app/routers/recipes.py
@router.get('/')
async def list_recipes(
    params: RecipeListParams = Depends(),
    db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)
):
    query = select(Recipe).where(Recipe.is_published == True)

    if params.category_slug:
        query = query.join(Category).where(Category.slug == params.category_slug)
    if params.max_cook_time is not None:
        query = query.where(Recipe.cook_time_min <= params.max_cook_time)
    if params.difficulty:
        query = query.where(Recipe.difficulty == params.difficulty)
    if params.cursor:
        cursor_recipe = await db.get(Recipe, params.cursor)
        if cursor_recipe:
            query = query.where(Recipe.created_at < cursor_recipe.created_at)

    query = query.order_by(Recipe.created_at.desc()).limit(params.limit + 1)
    results = (await db.execute(query)).scalars().all()

    has_next = len(results) > params.limit
    recipes = results[:params.limit]
    next_cursor = str(recipes[-1].id) if has_next else None

    return {
        'recipes': [RecipeResponse.model_validate(r) for r in recipes],
        'next_cursor': next_cursor
    }
```

The cursor is the UUID of the last item — we fetch `limit + 1` rows to detect if there's a next page. Filters are composable and all optional. The `category_slug` filter joins on the categories table using the slug for clean URLs.

nodes._session: Session 4 — Image Upload Exploration (May 4, feat/image-upload branch, forked from n-17)
nodes.content: Recipes need image uploads — at least a hero image and optionally step-by-step photos. What are our best options for a small project like this?

Two solid approaches for image handling:

**Option A — AWS S3 with presigned URLs:**
- Backend generates a presigned PUT URL; frontend uploads directly to S3.
- Pros: Full control, cheap storage, scales infinitely.
- Cons: You manage resize/optimization yourself (usually via Lambda), need CloudFront for CDN, more operational overhead.

**Option B — Cloudinary:**
- Upload via their SDK, get back a URL with on-the-fly transforms.
- Pros: Built-in resize, crop, format conversion (WebP/AVIF), CDN included. One SDK call.
- Cons: Usage-based pricing, vendor lock-in on URLs.

For a project this size, I'd lean toward Cloudinary — it eliminates the Lambda resize pipeline and gives you responsive image transforms for free. But let's prototype both so you can decide with real code.

nodes._session: Session 4a — S3 Spike (May 4, spike/s3-upload branch, forked from n-19)
nodes.content: Let's prototype the S3 presigned URL approach first. Show me the upload flow.
We need image resizing too — generate thumbnails (200x200) and medium (800x600) versions automatically after upload. I guess that means Lambda triggers?

Yes, here's the resize pipeline with Lambda:

```python
# lambda/resize.py — deployed as AWS Lambda
import boto3
from PIL import Image
import io

SIZES = {
    'thumb': (200, 200),
    'medium': (800, 600),
}

def handler(event, context):
    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
    bucket = event['Records'][0]['s3']['bucket']['name']
    key = event['Records'][0]['s3']['object']['key']
    
    obj = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
    img = Image.open(io.BytesIO(obj['Body'].read()))
    
    for suffix, size in SIZES.items():
        resized = img.copy()
        resized.thumbnail(size, Image.LANCZOS)
        buffer = io.BytesIO()
        resized.save(buffer, format='WEBP', quality=85)
        buffer.seek(0)
        
        new_key = key.rsplit('.', 1)[0] + f'_{suffix}.webp'
        s3.put_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=new_key, Body=buffer, ContentType='image/webp')
```

**Infrastructure needed:**
- S3 bucket event notification → Lambda trigger on `ObjectCreated`
- Lambda IAM role with `s3:GetObject` and `s3:PutObject`
- Lambda layer with Pillow (or use a container image)
- CloudFront distribution for serving

This works but adds significant operational complexity. You need to handle Lambda cold starts (first resize can take 3-5s), configure dead-letter queues for failed resizes, and monitor the pipeline. For a small project, this might be more infrastructure than it's worth.

nodes._session: Session 4b — Cloudinary Spike (May 4, spike/cloudinary-upload branch, forked from n-19)
nodes.content: Let's try the Cloudinary approach instead. Show me upload with automatic resize transforms.

Cloudinary integration — upload, transform, and serve in one flow:

```python
# app/services/image.py
import cloudinary
import cloudinary.uploader

cloudinary.config(
    cloud_name=settings.cloudinary_cloud,
    api_key=settings.cloudinary_key,
    api_secret=settings.cloudinary_secret
)

async def upload_recipe_image(file: UploadFile, user_id: str) -> dict:
    result = cloudinary.uploader.upload(
        file.file,
        folder=f'recipebox/{user_id}',
        resource_type='image',
        allowed_formats=['jpg', 'png', 'webp'],
        transformation=[
            {'quality': 'auto', 'fetch_format': 'auto'}
        ]
    )
    return {
        'public_id': result['public_id'],
        'url': result['secure_url'],
        'thumb_url': cloudinary.utils.cloudinary_url(
            result['public_id'], width=200, height=200, crop='fill'
        )[0],
        'medium_url': cloudinary.utils.cloudinary_url(
            result['public_id'], width=800, height=600, crop='limit'
        )[0]
    }

# app/routers/uploads.py
@router.post('/images')
async def upload_image(
    file: UploadFile,
    user: User = Depends(get_current_user)
):
    if file.content_type not in ('image/jpeg', 'image/png', 'image/webp'):
        raise HTTPException(400, 'Unsupported image type')
    return await upload_recipe_image(file, str(user.id))
```

No Lambda, no S3 event triggers, no resize pipeline. Cloudinary handles format conversion (auto WebP/AVIF), quality optimization, resizing via URL transforms, and CDN delivery. You just store the `public_id` in your database.
This is much simpler. Let's finalize it with proper error handling and add an image_url field to the recipes table.

Final Cloudinary implementation with error handling and schema update:

```sql
-- Migration: add image fields to recipes
ALTER TABLE recipes ADD COLUMN image_public_id TEXT;
ALTER TABLE recipes ADD COLUMN image_url TEXT;
```

```python
# app/services/image.py (updated with error handling)
async def upload_recipe_image(file: UploadFile, user_id: str) -> dict:
    try:
        if file.size > 10 * 1024 * 1024:  # 10 MB limit
            raise ValueError('Image too large (max 10MB)')
        
        result = cloudinary.uploader.upload(
            file.file,
            folder=f'recipebox/{user_id}',
            resource_type='image',
            allowed_formats=['jpg', 'png', 'webp'],
            max_bytes=10_000_000,
            transformation=[{'quality': 'auto', 'fetch_format': 'auto'}]
        )
        return {
            'public_id': result['public_id'],
            'url': result['secure_url'],
            'thumb_url': cloudinary_url(result['public_id'], width=200, height=200, crop='fill')[0],
            'medium_url': cloudinary_url(result['public_id'], width=800, height=600, crop='limit')[0]
        }
    except cloudinary.exceptions.Error as e:
        raise HTTPException(502, f'Image service error: {e}')

async def delete_recipe_image(public_id: str):
    try:
        cloudinary.uploader.destroy(public_id)
    except cloudinary.exceptions.Error:
        pass  # Log but don't fail — orphaned images are cleaned up by scheduled job
```

The recipe create/update endpoints now accept `image_public_id` and store it alongside the computed `image_url`. On recipe deletion, we call `delete_recipe_image` to clean up. Decision: **going with Cloudinary** over S3 — 80% less code, no Lambda pipeline, and the free tier covers our initial scale.

nodes._session: Session 5 — Search (May 5, feat/search branch, forked from n-07)
nodes.content: Now let's add full-text search for recipes. Users should be able to search by recipe title, ingredient names, and description. I want it to feel fast and relevant.

PostgreSQL full-text search across recipe fields:

```sql
-- Add tsvector column for combined search
ALTER TABLE recipes ADD COLUMN search_tsv tsvector
    GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title, '')), 'A') ||
        setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(description, '')), 'B')
    ) STORED;

CREATE INDEX idx_recipe_search ON recipes USING gin(search_tsv);
```

```python
# app/routers/search.py
@router.get('/search')
async def search_recipes(
    q: str = Query(min_length=2, max_length=200),
    limit: int = Query(default=20, ge=1, le=50),
    db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)
):
    tsquery = func.websearch_to_tsquery('english', q)
    
    query = (
        select(
            Recipe,
            func.ts_rank(Recipe.search_tsv, tsquery).label('rank')
        )
        .where(Recipe.search_tsv.op('@@')(tsquery))
        .where(Recipe.is_published == True)
        .order_by(text('rank DESC'), Recipe.created_at.desc())
        .limit(limit)
    )
    
    results = (await db.execute(query)).all()
    return {
        'results': [
            {**RecipeResponse.model_validate(recipe).model_dump(), 'relevance': rank}
            for recipe, rank in results
        ],
        'query': q,
        'count': len(results)
    }
```

The `search_tsv` column is a generated tsvector that combines title (weight A, highest) and description (weight B). The GIN index makes `@@` matching fast even at scale. `websearch_to_tsquery` supports Google-style syntax: `chicken OR beef`, `"pasta salad"`, `-spicy`.

To also search by ingredient name, we'd need a subquery join — but for v1 this title+description search covers the main use case.
Add autocomplete suggestions that appear as the user types. It should be fast and fuzzy — handle typos like 'chiken' matching 'chicken'.

Trigram-based autocomplete with fuzzy matching:

```sql
-- Enable the extension
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm;

-- GIN index on recipe titles for trigram similarity
CREATE INDEX idx_recipe_title_trgm ON recipes USING gin(title gin_trgm_ops);
```

```python
# app/routers/search.py
@router.get('/autocomplete')
async def autocomplete(
    q: str = Query(min_length=1, max_length=100),
    limit: int = Query(default=5, ge=1, le=10),
    db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db)
):
    similarity = func.similarity(Recipe.title, q)
    
    query = (
        select(Recipe.id, Recipe.title, similarity.label('sim'))
        .where(Recipe.is_published == True)
        .where(similarity > 0.1)
        .order_by(similarity.desc())
        .limit(limit)
    )
    
    results = (await db.execute(query)).all()
    return {
        'suggestions': [
            {'id': str(r.id), 'title': r.title, 'score': round(r.sim, 3)}
            for r in results
        ]
    }
```

`pg_trgm` breaks strings into 3-character grams and compares overlap. 'chiken' shares most trigrams with 'chicken' (chi, hic, ick, cke, ken) so it gets a high similarity score. The GIN index makes this O(log n) instead of a full table scan. For the frontend, debounce the input by 200ms and call `/autocomplete?q=...` on each keystroke.

nodes._session: Session 6 — Integration (May 6, back on main branch)
nodes.content: I've been building features on separate branches. Can you compare the auth branch and recipe-crud branch to see how they diverge from main?

Here's the divergence report between feat/auth and feat/recipe-crud:

**Lowest Common Ancestor:** n-07 (the final schema message on main — "Updated schema with recipe_ingredients and reviews")

**feat/auth has 6 unique nodes:**
- n-08: JWT + bcrypt auth request
- n-09: Auth module implementation
- n-10: Email verification request
- n-11: Email verification implementation
- n-12: Google OAuth request
- n-13: Google OAuth implementation

**feat/recipe-crud has 4 unique nodes:**
- n-14: Recipe CRUD request
- n-15: CRUD endpoints implementation
- n-16: Pagination request
- n-17: Cursor pagination implementation

**Conflict analysis:** No schema conflicts. Auth added `is_verified`, `verification_token`, `oauth_provider`, `oauth_provider_id` columns to users. Recipe CRUD didn't touch the users table. Both branches read from the same base schema. Safe to merge sequentially — auth first, then recipe-crud.
Great. Merge them all into main — auth, recipe CRUD, the Cloudinary image upload, and search. Give me the final integrated project overview.

All features merged. Here's the final RecipeBox project overview:

**Database (8 tables):**
- `users` — with password auth, email verification, Google OAuth support
- `categories` — recipe categories with slugs
- `recipes` — with image_public_id (Cloudinary), search_tsv (FTS), all metadata
- `ingredients` — ingredient catalog
- `recipe_ingredients` — junction with quantity/unit/order
- `reviews` — 1-5 star ratings with comments
- Plus: GIN indexes for FTS and trigram autocomplete

**API Endpoints (4 routers, 14 endpoints):**
- `POST /signup`, `POST /login`, `GET /me`, `GET /login/google`, `GET /callback/google`, `GET /verify`
- `GET /recipes`, `POST /recipes`, `GET /recipes/{id}`, `PUT /recipes/{id}`, `DELETE /recipes/{id}`
- `POST /images` (Cloudinary upload)
- `GET /search?q=`, `GET /autocomplete?q=`

**Architecture decisions made:**
1. Cloudinary over S3 — simpler pipeline, sufficient for our scale (spike/s3-upload archived)
2. Cursor-based over offset pagination — stable under concurrent writes
3. PostgreSQL FTS over Elasticsearch — good enough for v1, no extra infra
4. JWT with 30min expiry, no refresh tokens yet — add in v2

**What's next for v2:** Refresh tokens, ingredient-level search, recipe collections/bookmarks, social features (following users, activity feed).

nodes._session: Summary node — condenses the auth session to save tokens
nodes.content: AUTH MODULE SUMMARY: Built complete authentication for RecipeBox with three flows: (1) Email+password signup/login using bcrypt hashing and JWT access tokens (30min expiry), with /signup, /login, and /me endpoints. (2) Email verification via token-based link — unverified users blocked from creating recipes via require_verified dependency. (3) Google OAuth via authlib — users signing in with Google are auto-verified, accounts are linked by email if they already exist. Schema additions: is_verified, verification_token, token_expires_at, oauth_provider, oauth_provider_id columns on users table.

node_ancestry._note: Representative subset. The closure-table trigger auto-populates all pairs on INSERT. Format: [ancestor_id, descendant_id, depth]

context_pins._why: The schema definition is critical context for every feature branch — pinned with high priority so it's always included in context assembly
context_pins.reason: Core DB schema — always needed when building features that touch the database
Core DB schema — always needed when building features that touch the database
Core DB schema — need to know table structure to build search indexes
Core DB schema — need to know recipes table to add image columns

context_pins._why: The project scaffold is pinned on main so the integration session always has the directory structure in context
context_pins.reason: Project directory structure — reference when integrating features

context_imports._why: When building search, Alex imports the schema node to know the table structure and the recipe CRUD node to know the Pydantic models and endpoint patterns
When building Cloudinary integration, Alex imports the S3 spike comparison node to remember why S3 was rejected
When doing integration on main, Alex imports the Cloudinary decision node to reference the final image upload approach
Alex also imports the full search implementation subtree into main for integration

node_summaries._why: The auth session (6 nodes, ~1645 tokens) is summarized into a single 180-token node. When context is assembled for the main branch integration, the summary replaces the verbose originals, saving ~1465 tokens.
